does: This paper introduces SnapGuard, a lightweight detection system for identifying prompt injection attacks targeting screenshot-based web agents. The method operates by analyzing rendered webpages to distinguish malicious injected content from benign pages using both visual and textual characteristics. Rather than requiring expensive inference from large vision-language models, SnapGuard employs a more efficient approach that examines distinct patterns exhibited by compromised webpages. The core contribution is demonstrating that prompt injection attacks leave detectable signatures in screenshots that can be identified without substantial computational overhead, addressing a critical vulnerability in web automation systems that rely on visual page understanding rather than structured HTML representations.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research in web agent security and adversarial robustness in language models. It extends prior defenses against prompt injection attacks, which have primarily focused on text-based systems and structured data representations. The paper responds to limitations of existing multimodal detection approaches that leverage large vision-language models like GPT-4V or CLIP, which impose prohibitive computational costs. SnapGuard also connects to broader research on efficient machine learning and model compression techniques, as well as work on understanding how adversarial perturbations manifest differently across modalities. The screenshot-specific threat model complements previous studies on web agent vulnerabilities and establishes new challenges distinct from traditional text-only injection scenarios.
enables: This work enables the deployment of web agents in resource-constrained environments where computational efficiency is critical, such as edge devices or systems with limited GPU memory. By reducing the detection overhead, SnapGuard makes it practical to monitor web agent interactions continuously without sacrificing performance. The lightweight detection framework opens possibilities for real-time defense mechanisms embedded directly in agent systems. Future work could build on the identified visual and textual signatures of injections to develop adaptive attacks that evade lightweight detection, spurring an iterative improvement cycle. Additionally, the approach may generalize to other screenshot-based AI systems beyond web agents, and could inspire similar efficient detection methods for other multimodal security threats in autonomous systems.
